topics.title: Database Normalization
topics.description: Normalization is the process of organizing a relational database to reduce data redundancy and improve data integrity. Normal forms are progressive rules: 1NF eliminates repeating groups and ensures atomic values. 2NF eliminates partial dependencies (non-key columns that depend on only part of a composite key). 3NF eliminates transitive dependencies (non-key columns that depend on other non-key columns). BCNF (Boyce-Codd NF) is a stricter version of 3NF. Denormalization intentionally introduces redundancy for query performance — common in read-heavy or analytics workloads.
topics.story: Imagine a single massive Excel spreadsheet tracking orders: OrderID, CustomerName, CustomerEmail, CustomerCity, ProductName, ProductCategory, ProductPrice, Quantity, OrderDate. Every time a customer changes their email, you update it in 500 order rows — and miss one. Now two rows show different emails for the same customer. That's the update anomaly. If you delete the last order for a customer, you lose their contact details — that's the deletion anomaly. Normalization splits this into Customers, Products, and Orders tables — each fact is stored exactly once. Now updating Alice's email is one row in Customers, and the truth is consistent everywhere.
topics.analogy: 1NF = no list in a cell (not 'Red, Blue, Green' in one column — use separate rows). 2NF = each column depends on the WHOLE primary key, not part of it (if PK is (OrderID, ProductID), the product's name depends only on ProductID, not the full pair — move it out). 3NF = columns depend on the key, the whole key, and nothing but the key (zip code → city is a non-key-to-non-key dependency — move city to a zip code table). Denormalization = adding a TotalOrderAmount column that's computed from line items — redundant but avoids an expensive JOIN on every read.
topics.memoryAnchor: 1NF: atomic values, no repeating groups, each row unique (has a PK). 2NF: all non-key cols depend on the FULL composite PK (no partial deps). 3NF: no transitive deps (non-key → non-key). BCNF: every determinant is a candidate key. Anomalies that normalization fixes: Update (change in one row, not others), Insert (can't add a fact without related data), Delete (removing a row destroys unrelated facts). Functional dependency: A → B means knowing A uniquely determines B. Candidate key: minimal set of columns that uniquely identifies a row. Denormalization trade-offs: faster reads, slower writes, risk of inconsistency.
topics.firstPrinciples: A relation is in 1NF if every attribute value is atomic and each row is unique. A relation is in 2NF if it is in 1NF and every non-prime attribute is fully functionally dependent on every candidate key — no attribute depends on just part of a composite key. A relation is in 3NF if it is in 2NF and no non-prime attribute transitively depends on a candidate key — i.e., no non-prime → non-prime dependency. BCNF requires every determinant to be a candidate key (stricter than 3NF — removes anomalies even when overlapping candidate keys exist). In practice, normalize to 3NF, then selectively denormalize where query performance demands.
topics.bruteForce: One giant table (flat file) with all data: Order_ID, Cust_Name, Cust_Email, Prod_Name, Prod_Price, Qty, Order_Date. Update anomaly: 1000 rows need email updated. Insert anomaly: can't add product without an order. Delete anomaly: delete last order → lose customer. Massive storage waste: product name repeated in every order row.
topics.optimizedApproach: Normalize to 3NF for OLTP: Customers(id, name, email, city), Products(id, name, category, price), Orders(id, customer_id, order_date), OrderItems(order_id, product_id, qty, unit_price). unit_price captured at order time (snapshot) — product price can change later. For reporting/analytics: create denormalized star schema (fact + dimension tables) or materialized views. Partial denormalization: store category_name in products table rather than a categories join if it rarely changes and is frequently queried.
topics.whenToUse: Normalize (3NF): OLTP systems — banking, e-commerce, CRM — where writes and correctness matter. Denormalize: OLAP, data warehouses, reporting dashboards — where reads dominate. Hybrid: OLTP-normalized core with Redis/materialized view caches for heavy read paths. Always ask: 'Will this change frequently?' — if yes, normalize. 'Will this be queried 10,000 times/second?' — if yes, consider denormalization.

examples.title: 1NF → 2NF → 3NF Step-by-Step
examples.code:
-- ─── UNNORMALIZED (UNF) ─────────────────────────────────────────────
-- One table with all order data, repeating groups, non-atomic values
CREATE TABLE UNF_Orders (
    order_id      INT,
    customer_name VARCHAR(100),
    customer_email VARCHAR(100),
    customer_city  VARCHAR(50),
    -- Non-atomic: multiple products in one column (violates 1NF)
    products      VARCHAR(500), -- 'Laptop:999.99:1, Mouse:29.99:2'
    order_date    DATE
);

-- ─── 1NF: atomic values, no repeating groups ─────────────────────────
-- Fix: split products into separate rows, composite PK
CREATE TABLE Orders_1NF (
    order_id      INT,
    customer_name VARCHAR(100),
    customer_email VARCHAR(100),
    customer_city  VARCHAR(50),  -- depends on customer, not full PK (partial dep!)
    product_name  VARCHAR(100),  -- depends on product_id only (partial dep!)
    product_price DECIMAL(10,2),
    quantity      INT,
    order_date    DATE,          -- depends on order_id only (partial dep!)
    PRIMARY KEY (order_id, product_name)  -- composite PK
);
-- Problem: city depends on customer only; product_price depends on product only
-- => partial dependencies on composite PK => NOT in 2NF

-- ─── 2NF: remove partial dependencies ───────────────────────────────
-- Separate tables for each entity; each non-key column → whole key
CREATE TABLE Customers_2NF (
    customer_id   INT PRIMARY KEY,
    customer_name VARCHAR(100),
    customer_email VARCHAR(100),
    customer_city  VARCHAR(50)   -- still here: city depends on customer → OK
    -- BUT: city → state, zip → city (transitive!) => NOT in 3NF
);

CREATE TABLE Products_2NF (
    product_id    INT PRIMARY KEY,
    product_name  VARCHAR(100),
    product_price DECIMAL(10,2),
    category_name VARCHAR(50)   -- depends on product → OK for 2NF
    -- category_name → category_id (transitive dep) => NOT in 3NF
);

CREATE TABLE Orders_2NF (
    order_id    INT PRIMARY KEY,
    customer_id INT NOT NULL,
    order_date  DATE,
    FOREIGN KEY (customer_id) REFERENCES Customers_2NF(customer_id)
);

CREATE TABLE OrderItems_2NF (
    order_id   INT,
    product_id INT,
    quantity   INT,
    unit_price DECIMAL(10,2),  -- snapshot price at order time (not from products)
    PRIMARY KEY (order_id, product_id),
    FOREIGN KEY (order_id)   REFERENCES Orders_2NF(order_id),
    FOREIGN KEY (product_id) REFERENCES Products_2NF(product_id)
);

-- ─── 3NF: remove transitive dependencies ──────────────────────────────
-- Non-key → non-key dependencies removed:
-- (a) customer_city is OK IF it doesn't determine other non-keys
-- (b) category_name in Products creates transitive dep if categories have more data
CREATE TABLE Categories_3NF (
    category_id   INT PRIMARY KEY,
    category_name VARCHAR(50),
    description   TEXT
);

CREATE TABLE Products_3NF (
    product_id   INT PRIMARY KEY,
    product_name VARCHAR(100),
    price        DECIMAL(10,2),
    category_id  INT NOT NULL,        -- FK, not the name (no transitive dep)
    FOREIGN KEY (category_id) REFERENCES Categories_3NF(category_id)
);

CREATE TABLE Customers_3NF (
    customer_id INT PRIMARY KEY,
    name        VARCHAR(100),
    email       VARCHAR(100) UNIQUE,
    city        VARCHAR(50),
    zip_code    VARCHAR(10)
    -- zip_code → city would be transitive: fix by creating PostalCodes table
    -- In practice, tolerate this if city is never updated independently of customer
);

-- Final 3NF schema: Customers, Categories, Products, Orders, OrderItems
-- Each fact stored exactly once. Update email → 1 row. Add product → 1 row.
SELECT c.name, p.product_name, oi.quantity, oi.unit_price,
       cat.category_name, o.order_date
FROM   Customers_3NF c
JOIN   Orders_2NF    o   ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id
JOIN   OrderItems_2NF oi ON o.order_id    = oi.order_id
JOIN   Products_3NF  p  ON oi.product_id = p.product_id
JOIN   Categories_3NF cat ON p.category_id = cat.category_id
WHERE  c.customer_id = 42;
examples.explanation: The progression shows what each normal form removes: 1NF removes non-atomic values and repeating groups. 2NF removes partial dependencies — any column that depends on only PART of a composite primary key gets its own table. 3NF removes transitive dependencies — if A→B and B→C where B is a non-key, then C should be in its own table. The unit_price in OrderItems is a deliberate snapshot (denormalization) — you need the price at time of order, not today's price.
examples.realWorldUse: E-commerce: exactly this Orders/OrderItems/Products/Customers schema. The unit_price snapshot pattern is critical — Amazon's order history shows the price you paid, not today's price. JPA/Hibernate @OneToMany, @ManyToOne map exactly to these FK relationships. @JoinColumn(name='customer_id') maps to the FK column. Spring Data JPA generates the JOIN queries from JPQL or method names.

examples.title: Anomalies and Denormalization
examples.code:
-- ─── The 3 Anomalies in Unnormalized Tables ─────────────────────────

-- Unnormalized course enrollment table
CREATE TABLE UNF_Enrollment (
    student_id   INT,
    student_name VARCHAR(100),
    student_email VARCHAR(100),  -- transitive: student_id → email
    course_id    INT,
    course_name  VARCHAR(100),   -- transitive: course_id → course_name
    instructor   VARCHAR(100),   -- transitive: course_id → instructor
    room         VARCHAR(20),    -- transitive: course_id → room
    grade        CHAR(1),        -- depends on (student_id, course_id) ← OK
    PRIMARY KEY (student_id, course_id)
);

-- INSERT ANOMALY: can't add a new course without a student enrolled
-- INSERT INTO UNF_Enrollment VALUES (NULL, NULL, NULL, 201, 'Algorithms', 'Prof.Smith', 'B101', NULL);
-- ^ violates NOT NULL or PK constraint

-- UPDATE ANOMALY: rename course → update 1000 rows
-- UPDATE UNF_Enrollment SET course_name='Advanced Algorithms' WHERE course_id=201;
-- If any row is missed → inconsistent data

-- DELETE ANOMALY: remove last student from course → lose course info
-- DELETE FROM UNF_Enrollment WHERE course_id=201 AND student_id=42;
-- If student 42 was the only one in course 201 → course data gone!

-- ─── 3NF Solution: separate tables ───────────────────────────────────
CREATE TABLE Students (
    student_id  INT PRIMARY KEY,
    name        VARCHAR(100),
    email       VARCHAR(100) UNIQUE
);

CREATE TABLE Courses (
    course_id   INT PRIMARY KEY,
    course_name VARCHAR(100),
    instructor  VARCHAR(100),
    room        VARCHAR(20)
);

CREATE TABLE Enrollments (
    student_id  INT,
    course_id   INT,
    grade       CHAR(1),
    enrolled_at DATE,
    PRIMARY KEY (student_id, course_id),
    FOREIGN KEY (student_id) REFERENCES Students(student_id),
    FOREIGN KEY (course_id)  REFERENCES Courses(course_id)
);

-- Add course without students:
INSERT INTO Courses VALUES (202, 'Machine Learning', 'Prof. Lee', 'C305');
-- Rename course → 1 row:
UPDATE Courses SET course_name = 'Advanced ML' WHERE course_id = 202;
-- Drop enrollment without losing course:
DELETE FROM Enrollments WHERE student_id=42 AND course_id=201;

-- ─── Denormalization for Performance ─────────────────────────────────
-- OLAP/Reporting: avoid JOINs for dashboard queries

-- Materialized summary (denormalized)
CREATE TABLE Student_Course_Summary (
    student_id     INT,
    student_name   VARCHAR(100),  -- duplicated from Students
    course_name    VARCHAR(100),  -- duplicated from Courses
    instructor     VARCHAR(100),  -- duplicated from Courses
    grade          CHAR(1),
    gpa_equivalent DECIMAL(3,2), -- computed, stored redundantly
    PRIMARY KEY (student_id, course_name)
);
-- Trade-off: update student name → also update this table
-- Benefit: report query is a single table scan, no JOINs

-- When to denormalize:
-- 1. Dashboard/report queries JOIN 5+ tables and run 10,000/day
-- 2. Data is read-heavy and rarely updated
-- 3. Historical snapshots where data shouldn't change with source

-- Query comparison:
-- Normalized (3 joins, indexes required):
SELECT s.name, c.course_name, e.grade
FROM   Students s
JOIN   Enrollments e ON s.student_id = e.student_id
JOIN   Courses c     ON e.course_id  = c.course_id
WHERE  s.student_id = 42;

-- Denormalized (single table, cache-friendly):
SELECT student_name, course_name, grade
FROM   Student_Course_Summary
WHERE  student_id = 42;
examples.explanation: The three anomalies make unnormalized tables painful to maintain: insert anomaly (can't record partial facts), update anomaly (must update in multiple rows consistently), delete anomaly (removing one fact destroys unrelated facts). 3NF eliminates all three. The denormalization section shows the deliberate exception: when a JOIN-heavy query runs millions of times, storing redundant data in a summary table (or materialized view) is a justified trade-off. MySQL supports materialized views manually (via triggers or batch jobs); PostgreSQL 9.3+ has native MATERIALIZED VIEW.
examples.realWorldUse: Netflix uses a highly denormalized Cassandra schema for recommendations — reading one row gives all data for a UI panel with no joins. MySQL OLTP databases are normalized (3NF) but surrounded by Redis caches and Elasticsearch indexes that are denormalized copies for fast reads. The trade-off is consistency: when the source changes, the caches need invalidation. Spring's @Cacheable + cache eviction policies automate this pattern.

problems.title: Identify Normal Form Violations
problems.description: Given this table: EMPLOYEE(emp_id, emp_name, dept_id, dept_name, dept_location, project_id, project_name, hours_worked). Primary key is (emp_id, project_id). Identify: (a) which 1NF rules are satisfied, (b) which partial dependencies exist that violate 2NF, (c) which transitive dependencies violate 3NF. Then write the normalized 3NF schema.
problems.hint1: 1NF check: are all values atomic? Does the table have a PK? No repeating groups?
problems.hint2: 2NF partial dep: emp_name, dept_id, dept_name, dept_location depend on emp_id alone (not the full PK). project_name depends on project_id alone.
problems.hint3: 3NF transitive: dept_id → dept_name, dept_location. This chain: emp_id → dept_id → dept_name means dept_name transitively depends on emp_id through dept_id.
problems.testCases:
1NF: satisfied
2NF violations: emp_id→emp_name/dept_*, project_id→project_name
3NF violation: emp_id→dept_id→dept_name (transitive)
3NF tables: DEPARTMENTS, EMPLOYEES, PROJECTS, ASSIGNMENTS

problems.title: Design a Normalized Schema for a Library System
problems.description: Design a 3NF database schema for a library. Requirements: Books have a title, ISBN, publication year, and one or more authors. Books belong to exactly one genre. Members can borrow books. Each loan has a borrow date and return date. A member can have multiple active loans. Write the CREATE TABLE statements with proper PKs, FKs, and constraints.
problems.hint1: Book and Author have a many-to-many relationship — needs a junction table (book_authors).
problems.hint2: Genre is a separate table (not just a string in books) — avoids update anomaly when genre name changes.
problems.hint3: Loans table: (member_id, book_id, borrow_date) as PK, or surrogate loan_id. Return date is nullable (NULL = not returned yet).
problems.starterCode:
-- Design the 3NF schema for a library system
-- Requirements:
-- - Books: title, ISBN, publication year, 1+ authors
-- - Books belong to exactly one genre
-- - Members can borrow books
-- - Each loan has borrow date and return date (null if not returned)

-- Write CREATE TABLE statements below:

-- TODO: Genres table

-- TODO: Authors table

-- TODO: Books table (with genre FK)

-- TODO: Book_Authors junction table

-- TODO: Members table

-- TODO: Loans table

-- Sample queries:
-- Find all books by author 'Joshua Bloch'
-- Find all currently borrowed books for member 42
-- Find overdue loans (return_date IS NULL AND borrow_date < 14 days ago)
problems.solutionCode:
-- ─── 3NF Library Schema ──────────────────────────────────────────────

CREATE TABLE genres (
    genre_id   INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY,
    genre_name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL UNIQUE
);
-- Separate table: genre rename updates 1 row, not all books

CREATE TABLE authors (
    author_id  INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY,
    first_name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    last_name  VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    UNIQUE KEY uq_author (first_name, last_name)
);

CREATE TABLE books (
    book_id    INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY,
    isbn       VARCHAR(13) NOT NULL UNIQUE,
    title      VARCHAR(200) NOT NULL,
    pub_year   YEAR,
    genre_id   INT NOT NULL,
    FOREIGN KEY (genre_id) REFERENCES genres(genre_id)
        ON UPDATE CASCADE   -- rename genre → books auto-updated via FK
);
-- isbn UNIQUE: one record per physical book edition
-- genre_id FK: books belong to exactly one genre (1:M)

CREATE TABLE book_authors (
    book_id   INT NOT NULL,
    author_id INT NOT NULL,
    author_order TINYINT DEFAULT 1,  -- 1=primary, 2=co-author
    PRIMARY KEY (book_id, author_id),
    FOREIGN KEY (book_id)   REFERENCES books(book_id)   ON DELETE CASCADE,
    FOREIGN KEY (author_id) REFERENCES authors(author_id) ON DELETE RESTRICT
);
-- Junction table: books M:M authors
-- ON DELETE CASCADE: remove book → remove its authorship links
-- ON DELETE RESTRICT: block author delete if they have books

CREATE TABLE members (
    member_id INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY,
    name      VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    email     VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL UNIQUE,
    joined_at DATE DEFAULT (CURRENT_DATE)
);

CREATE TABLE loans (
    loan_id     INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY,
    book_id     INT NOT NULL,
    member_id   INT NOT NULL,
    borrow_date DATE NOT NULL DEFAULT (CURRENT_DATE),
    return_date DATE NULL,  -- NULL means still on loan
    FOREIGN KEY (book_id)   REFERENCES books(book_id),
    FOREIGN KEY (member_id) REFERENCES members(member_id),
    INDEX idx_loans_member (member_id),
    INDEX idx_loans_book   (book_id),
    INDEX idx_loans_overdue (return_date, borrow_date)  -- for overdue queries
);

-- ─── Sample Queries ───────────────────────────────────────────────────

-- Books by Joshua Bloch:
SELECT b.title, b.pub_year
FROM   books b
JOIN   book_authors ba ON b.book_id  = ba.book_id
JOIN   authors a       ON ba.author_id = a.author_id
WHERE  a.first_name = 'Joshua' AND a.last_name = 'Bloch'
ORDER BY b.pub_year;

-- Active loans for member 42:
SELECT b.title, b.isbn, l.borrow_date
FROM   loans l
JOIN   books b ON l.book_id = b.book_id
WHERE  l.member_id = 42 AND l.return_date IS NULL
ORDER BY l.borrow_date;

-- Overdue loans (not returned after 14 days):
SELECT m.name, m.email, b.title,
       l.borrow_date,
       DATEDIFF(CURRENT_DATE, l.borrow_date) AS days_overdue
FROM   loans l
JOIN   members m ON l.member_id = m.member_id
JOIN   books   b ON l.book_id   = b.book_id
WHERE  l.return_date IS NULL
  AND  l.borrow_date < DATE_SUB(CURRENT_DATE, INTERVAL 14 DAY)
ORDER BY days_overdue DESC;
problems.testCases:
Tables: genres, authors, books, book_authors, members, loans
book_authors is M:M junction
loans.return_date IS NULL = currently borrowed
Overdue = borrow_date older than 14 days AND return_date IS NULL
